lecture_title: How a Car Engine Works: Mechanics and Dynamics

nodes.label: The Four-Stroke Engine Cycle
nodes.summary: The core operational cycle of an internal combustion engine, consisting of intake, compression, power, and exhaust strokes to translate fuel combustion into mechanical energy.

nodes.label: Engine Block and Head Structure
nodes.summary: The structural foundation of the engine split into a lower block holding pistons and the crankshaft, and an upper head housing the valves, ports, and ignition systems.

nodes.label: Engine Configurations and Sizes
nodes.summary: Cylinders are arranged in inline, V-shape, or flat/boxer layouts, with displacement measuring the total cylinder volume in liters to dictate baseline engine power.

nodes.label: Torque versus Horsepower
nodes.summary: Torque represents the actual physical rotational strength of the crankshaft measured in pound-feet, whereas horsepower is a calculated value of torque multiplied by engine RPM over time.

nodes.label: Essential Supporting Sub-Systems
nodes.summary: Critical auxiliary pipelines including air intake, pressurized fuel delivery, liquid cooling, electrical charging/ignition, and continuous oil lubrication that prevent mechanical failure.

nodes.label: Valvetrain Mechanics & Variable Timing
nodes.summary: The breathing apparatus of the engine where camshafts actuate intake and exhaust valves, optimized dynamically across RPM bands via smart timing adjustment systems like VVT-i.

nodes.label: Atkinson Cycle Engine Design
nodes.summary: An efficiency-focused combustion method that leaves intake valves open during early compression to minimize pumping energy losses, commonly paired with hybrid electric motors.
